# Development Tools

This document describes the recommended tools for working with this project, particularly for AI coding agents and developers.

## Tool Location

Recommended tools are installed in `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`:

```bash
# List available tools
ls -la ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/

# Add to PATH (if not already in ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc)
export PATH="$HOME/.local/softwarewrighter/bin:$PATH"
```

## Tool Help Convention

**IMPORTANT**: All tools in `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/` support `--help` with AI coding agent-specific sections. These extended help sections provide additional context, examples, and best practices tailored for AI agents.

```bash
# Standard help (short)
tool-name -h

# Extended help with AI sections (recommended)
tool-name --help
```

## Core Tools

### 1. proact - AI Agent Documentation Generator

**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/proact
**Version**: 0.1.0

**Purpose**: Generates comprehensive AI coding agent documentation for projects

**When to Use**:
- When starting work on a new project
- When you want standardized AI agent instructions
- When docs/learnings.md needs to be initialized or updated
- When project processes/standards have changed

**Installation**:
```bash
# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
```

**Usage**:
```bash
# Generate docs for current project
proact .

# Generate docs for specific project
proact /path/to/project

# Custom output directory
proact /path/to/project -o documentation

# Preview what would be generated (dry-run)
proact . --dry-run

# Verbose output
proact . --verbose
```

**What It Generates**:
- `docs/ai_agent_instructions.md` - Comprehensive AI coding agent guidelines
  - Process-oriented workflow
  - Quality standards
  - Testing strategies
  - Tech debt avoidance
  - Playwright MCP setup
  - Project-specific guidelines (Rust, WASM, etc.)
- `docs/learnings.md` - Template for capturing issues and solutions
  - Common patterns that cause problems
  - Proactive prevention strategies
  - Root cause analysis examples

**Key Features**:
- Enforces checkpoint process (tests, linting, formatting, docs, git)
- Includes Playwright MCP setup instructions
- Project-specific guidelines based on technology detected
- Continuous improvement focus
- Tech debt limits (file size, TODO count, etc.)

**AI Agent Notes**:
When proact generates documentation for a project, it creates a baseline. You should:
1. Read the generated docs/ai_agent_instructions.md
2. Follow the checkpoint process it describes
3. Update docs/learnings.md when you encounter issues
4. Run proact again if project structure changes significantly

### 2. markdown-checker - Markdown Validation Tool

**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/markdown-checker
**Version**: 0.1.0

**Purpose**: Ensures markdown files use ASCII-only characters for maximum portability

**When to Use**:
- BEFORE committing any markdown changes (part of pre-commit process)
- After editing README.md or any docs/*.md files
- When preparing documentation for GitHub (web preview compatibility)
- To fix Unicode characters (arrows, emojis, box-drawing)

**Installation**:
```bash
# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
```

**Usage**:
```bash
# Validate README.md in current directory
markdown-checker

# Validate specific file
markdown-checker -f CONTRIBUTING.md

# Validate all markdown in directory
markdown-checker -f "*.md"

# Validate recursively (all markdown in docs/)
markdown-checker -p docs -f "**/*.md"

# Auto-fix tree symbols
markdown-checker --fix

# Preview fixes without applying
markdown-checker --dry-run

# Verbose output
markdown-checker -v
```

**Validation Rules**:
- [x] ASCII characters (32-126)
- [x] Whitespace (space, tab, LF, CR)
- [x] Unicode arrows (-> becomes ->)
- [x] Emojis ([x] becomes [x] or remove)
- [x] Box-drawing characters (+|+- becomes +|-)

**Auto-Fixable**:
- Tree symbols: `+ -> +`, `| -> |`, `+ -> +`, `- -> -`

**Not Auto-Fixable** (manual intervention required):
- Unicode arrows (->)
- Emojis ([x] [x] [WIP])
- Accented characters (e a n)
- Other non-ASCII Unicode

**Why ASCII-Only?**:
- GitHub README web preview works correctly
- Compatible with all terminals and editors
- Works in CI/CD pipelines
- Accessible to screen readers
- No encoding issues

**Pre-Commit Integration**:
```bash
# Part of mandatory pre-commit process
markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"
# Fix any issues before committing
```

**AI Agent Notes**:
- ALWAYS run markdown-checker before committing documentation
- Use --fix for tree symbols, manually fix other Unicode
- If validation fails, FIX the issues (never skip this step)
- Common fixes:
  - `->` becomes `->`
  - `[x]` becomes `[x]` in checklists
  - `[WIP]` becomes `[WIP]` or remove
  - Box-drawing trees become ASCII trees

### 3. sw-install - Binary Installation Tool

**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/sw-install
**Version**: 0.1.0

**Purpose**: Installs compiled Rust binaries to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`

**When to Use**:
- First-time setup of development environment
- After building a new Rust CLI tool locally
- When updating an existing tool
- When you need a specific version of a tool

**Installation**:
```bash
# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
```

**First-Time Setup**:
```bash
# 1. Setup installation directory and PATH
sw-install --setup-install-dir

# 2. Reload shell configuration
source ~/.bashrc  # or ~/.zshrc
```

**Usage**:
```bash
# Install a binary from project (release build)
sw-install -p ~/projects/markdown-checker

# Install with custom name
sw-install -p ~/projects/ask -r ask-dev

# Install debug build (for development)
sw-install -p ~/projects/proact -d

# Uninstall a binary
sw-install -u markdown-checker

# Dry-run (preview what would happen)
sw-install -p ~/projects/ask --dry-run

# Verbose output
sw-install -p ~/projects/ask --verbose
```

**How It Works**:
1. Detects Rust project (looks for Cargo.toml)
2. Finds binary name from Cargo.toml [[bin]] section
3. Copies from `target/release/` or `target/debug/`
4. Installs to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`
5. Makes executable (chmod +x)

**PATH Configuration**:
After `--setup-install-dir`, adds to your shell RC file:
```bash
export PATH="$HOME/.local/softwarewrighter/bin:$PATH"
```

**AI Agent Notes**:
- Use sw-install when you build a new tool and want it available globally
- Prefer release builds for performance: `sw-install -p <path>`
- Use debug builds for development: `sw-install -p <path> -d`
- Check which tools are available: `ls ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`

### 5. sw-checklist - Software Wrighter Project Requirements Checker

### 6. favicon - Favicon Generator

**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/favicon
**Version**: 0.1.0

**Purpose**: Generates favicon.ico files for web projects

**When to Use**:
- Setting up a new web project that needs a favicon
- Creating placeholder favicons during development

**Installation**:

```

**Usage**:
```bash

# Generate red question mark on transparent background (placeholder)
# Run this in the directory where index.html is served (e.g., public/ or static/)

# Common options:

- Common locations: public/, static/, dist/, www/
- Replace placeholder favicons before production deployment
- Use distinctive colors/text for different environments (dev/staging/prod)
- The -T flag creates transparent backgrounds for better visual appearance

## Tool Discovery

When new tools are added to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`:

1. **List available tools**:
   ```bash
   ls -la ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
   ```

2. **Check tool help**:
   ```bash
   <tool-name> --help  # Extended help with AI sections
   <tool-name> -h      # Short help
   ```

3. **Test with dry-run** (if supported):
   ```bash
   <tool-name> --dry-run <args>
   ```

4. **Update this doc** when new useful tools are discovered

## Integration with This Project

### Pre-Commit Process

From docs/process.md, the following tools are used:

```bash
# Step 1: Tests
cargo test

# Step 2: Linting
cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings

# Step 3: Formatting
cargo fmt --all

# Step 4: Markdown validation (THIS IS WHERE markdown-checker IS USED)
markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"

# Step 6: Update documentation
# If issues found, update docs/learnings.md
# Can use proact to regenerate if major changes
```

### Documentation Workflow

```bash
# 1. Make code/doc changes
vim README.md

# 2. Validate markdown before commit
markdown-checker -f "README.md"

# 3. Fix any issues
# (manually replace Unicode, or use --fix for tree symbols)

# 4. If new patterns found, update docs/learnings.md

# 5. Optionally regenerate full docs
proact .

# 6. Commit changes
git commit -m "docs: Update README with new features"
```

## Best Practices

### For AI Coding Agents

1. **Always check tool help first**:
   ```bash
   <tool> --help  # Not -h, use --help for AI-specific sections
   ```

2. **Use dry-run mode when learning**:
   ```bash
   proact . --dry-run
   markdown-checker --dry-run
   ```

3. **Integrate into workflow**:
   - Use markdown-checker in pre-commit process (mandatory)
   - Use proact when starting new projects
   - Update docs/learnings.md when encountering issues

4. **Validate before committing**:
   ```bash
   # Always run before commit if markdown changed
   markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"
   ```

5. **Keep tools updated**:
   ```bash
   # Rebuild and reinstall tool
   cd ~/projects/markdown-checker
   cargo build --release
   sw-install -p .
   ```

### For Human Developers

1. **Setup PATH once**:
   ```bash
   sw-install --setup-install-dir
   source ~/.bashrc
   ```

2. **Install tools as needed**:
   ```bash
   # Clone and build
   git clone https://github.com/softwarewrighter/<tool>.git
   cd <tool>
   cargo build --release

   # Install globally
   sw-install -p .
   ```

3. **Use in pre-commit hooks** (future):
   ```bash
   # .git/hooks/pre-commit
   #!/bin/bash
   markdown-checker -f "**/*.md" || exit 1
   ```

## Troubleshooting

### Tool Not Found

```bash
# Check if tool exists
ls ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/

# Check if PATH is set
echo $PATH | grep softwarewrighter

# Re-run setup if needed
sw-install --setup-install-dir
source ~/.bashrc  # or ~/.zshrc
```

### markdown-checker Validation Failures

```bash
# See detailed errors
markdown-checker -v

# Try auto-fix for tree symbols
markdown-checker --fix

# For other Unicode, manually replace:
# -> with ->
# [x] with [x]
# [WIP] with [WIP] or remove
```

### Permission Denied

```bash
# Tools should be executable, but if not:
chmod +x ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/<tool-name>
```

## Future Tools

As new tools are added to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`, document them here:

**Template**:
```markdown
### Tool Name

**Purpose**: Brief description

**When to Use**:
- Use case 1
- Use case 2

**Usage**:
```bash
tool-name --help
tool-name <common-usage>
```

**AI Agent Notes**:
- Specific guidance for AI agents
- Integration with project workflow
```

1	
2	
3	
4	
5	How can I have named tuples in Rust?
6	
7	You’re effectively asking for:
8	
9	“How do I get tuple-like positional access and named fields in Rust?”
10	
11	This touches on data modeling choices, not just syntax. Rust deliberately separates:
12	
13	Tuples → positional, lightweight, anonymous
14	
15	Structs → named, explicit, extensible
16	
17	There is no built-in “named tuple” type like Python’s namedtuple or OCaml’s labeled tuples—but you can achieve equivalent (and often better) designs using a few patterns.
18	
19	🧠 Design Considerations
20	Before jumping into syntax, think about what you actually want:
21	
22	Requirement	Best Rust Construct
23	Named fields, readable	struct
24	Tuple-like compactness	tuple struct
25	Pattern matching + names	struct
26	Interop / lightweight	tuple or tuple struct
27	Python-style namedtuple	struct + destructuring
28	Rust pushes you toward clarity over brevity, so most “named tuple” use cases map cleanly to struct.
29	
30	✅ Option 1 — Regular Struct (Idiomatic Rust)
31	This is the canonical solution.
32	
33	struct Point {
34	    x: i32,
35	    y: i32,
36	}
37	
38	let p = Point { x: 10, y: 20 };
39	
40	println!("{}", p.x);
41	Why this is usually best
42	Self-documenting
43	
44	Works with pattern matching
45	
46	Easy to extend later
47	
48	Zero runtime cost
49	
50	Destructuring (tuple-like feel)
51	let Point { x, y } = p;
52	✅ Option 2 — Tuple Struct (Hybrid)
53	If you want tuple syntax + type name, use a tuple struct:
54	
55	struct Point(i32, i32);
56	
57	let p = Point(10, 20);
58	println!("{}", p.0);
59	Tradeoffs
60	✔ lightweight
61	
62	✔ newtype-friendly
63	
64	✘ no field names (.0, .1 only)
65	
66	✅ Option 3 — Struct + impl Accessors (Encapsulation)
67	If you want both names and controlled access:
68	
69	struct Point(i32, i32);
70	
71	impl Point {
72	    fn x(&self) -> i32 { self.0 }
73	    fn y(&self) -> i32 { self.1 }
74	}
75	
76	let p = Point(10, 20);
77	println!("{}", p.x());
78	This gives:
79	
80	tuple storage
81	
82	named API
83	
84	✅ Option 4 — Pattern Matching Alias (Closest to Named Tuple Feel)
85	You can destructure inline to simulate named tuples:
86	
87	let (x, y) = (10, 20);
88	println!("{x}, {y}");
89	Or:
90	
91	let point = (10, 20);
92	let (x, y) = point;
93	This is the closest to “named tuple variables” but:
94	
95	names exist only locally
96	
97	not part of the type
98	
99	⚙️ Option 5 — Macros / Crates (Python-style namedtuple)
100	There are crates that mimic named tuples:
101	
102	named_tuple
103	
104	tuple_struct
105	
106	derive_more
107	
108	Example (conceptually):
109	
110	named_tuple!(Point { x: i32, y: i32 });
111	
112	let p = Point { x: 1, y: 2 };
113	Tradeoffs
114	✔ concise
115	
116	✘ adds dependency
117	
118	✘ not idiomatic Rust
119	
120	✘ less flexible than structs
121	
122	🧩 Option 6 — Type Alias for Tuple (Not Truly Named)
123	type Point = (i32, i32);
124	Usage:
125	
126	let p: Point = (10, 20);
127	But:
128	
129	❌ no field names
130	
131	❌ no safety improvement
132	
133	🧠 Architectural Guidance
134	Given your broader work (compilers, VMs, IRs, etc.), here’s how to think about it:
135	
136	Use struct when:
137	Modeling AST nodes
138	
139	IR representations
140	
141	VM state
142	
143	Anything evolving over time
144	
145	Use tuple structs when:
146	Newtype wrappers (e.g., Address(u32))
147	
148	Performance-critical tight representations
149	
150	Use tuples when:
151	Temporary return values
152	
153	Small local transformations
154	
155	⚖️ Comparison Summary
156	Feature	Tuple	Tuple Struct	Struct
157	Named fields	❌	❌	✅
158	Lightweight	✅	✅	✅
159	Readability	❌	⚠️	✅
160	Extensible	❌	❌	✅
161	Pattern matching	✅	✅	✅
162	🚀 Practical Recommendation
163	For your use cases (compilers, IR, MLPL, COR24 tooling):
164	
165	Default to struct unless you have a very specific reason not to.
166	
167	If you want “named tuple ergonomics,” combine:
168	
169	struct
170	
171	destructuring
172	
173	short field names
174	
175	🧪 Example (Compiler IR Style)
176	struct Add {
177	    lhs: u32,
178	    rhs: u32,
179	}
180	
181	let Add { lhs, rhs } = node;
182	This gives you:
183	
184	named semantics
185	
186	tuple-like unpacking
187	
188	future extensibility (e.g., add metadata)
189	
190	If you want something closer to OCaml
191	Rust intentionally avoids:
192	
193	(x=1, y=2)
194	But you can approximate with:
195	
196	struct Point { x: i32, y: i32 }
197	and destructuring.
198	
199	👍 Bottom line
200	Rust doesn’t have “named tuples” because:
201	
202	struct is the named tuple—just more powerful.
203	
204	If you want, I can show:
205	
206	how to design IR nodes using struct vs tuple tradeoffs
207	
208	or how to build a macro that generates named-tuple-like types with zero boilerplate
209	
210	
211	Consider the following discussion and propose a corresponding Rust example 
212	imagine we have a verb that takes 4 inputs, it expects an x, y coordinate, as well as a color, and also a transparency, and it reuturns a Boolean result of success or failure. 
213	i will show you the signature of this verb:
214	 plot⠀⎛x y  color  transparency⎠⠀───‣⠀⎛successˀ⎠ 
215	⠀
216	marc f. — 2:22 PM
217	⠀
218	now lets mint a tuple variable:
219	 ▪coord₂ ───‣ ⎛x y⎠ 
220	
221	this tuple variable is actually a verb that pushes its own local vars onto the stack for us whenever invoked.
222	
223	note that subscript "2" that suffixes the name of the tuple variable, this is to inform both compiler and reader that this verb outputs 2 values.
224	
225	
226	side note:
227	ALL verbs will ALWAYS have the mapping operator (this guy: "───‣") in their signature.
228	so macros, functions, operators, etc. always have "───‣" in their signatures.
229	
230	
231	we could also have minted and initialized the coord₂ variable in one stroke this way:
232	▪coord₂ ───‣ ⎛x y⎠  ⟵  3 , 9
233	
234	
235	or seperately:
236	▪coord₂ ───‣ ⎛x y⎠
237	coord₂ ⟵ 3 , 9
238	
239	
240	because it is a true tuple var, we can easily do this:
241	a , b  ⟵  coord₂
242	
243	
244	
245	me thinks you get the idea.
246	⠀
247	⠀
248	now lets put all this to practical use.
249	
250	
251	watch the magic:
252	⠀
253	Mike@work — 2:24 PM
254	continue; brb
255	marc f. — 2:25 PM
256	⠀
257	coord₂  ⟵  3 , 9
258	
259	plot⎛coord₂ Red 50%⎠
260	⠀
261	
262	⠀
263	we can visually see that the verb plot is having FOUR values passed to it, even though only 3 names appear in the parm list of its invocation.
264	and we can have as much refinement of control/expression as we please.
265	for example, i could make a tuple that has the coordinate as well as the color, or with the transparency too.
266	so:
267	⠀
268	plot⎛point₄⎠
269	⠀
270	notice that i didn't have to overload the plot function.
271	i just use it willy-nilly.
272	Mike@work — 2:28 PM
273	or coord3 for x y z
274	
275	Thought for a couple of seconds
276	You want a Rust analogue of:
277	
278	plot ⎛x y color transparency⎠ ───‣ ⎛successˀ⎠
279	
280	coord₂ ───‣ ⎛x y⎠
281	coord₂ ⟵ 3 , 9
282	
283	plot⎛coord₂ Red 50%⎠
284	Rust does not have automatic tuple-splicing in function calls, so this will not work directly:
285	
286	plot(coord2, Color::Red, 0.5); // not the same as plot(x, y, color, alpha)
287	But Rust can model the idea cleanly with named tuple-like structs and a trait that converts different “point descriptions” into the canonical four plotting arguments.
288	
289	Rust version: tuple variables as “argument bundles”
290	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]

294	}

296	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
297	struct Coord3 {
298	    x: i32,
299	    y: i32,
300	    z: i32,

file.content:
How can I have named tuples in Rust?

You’re effectively asking for:

“How do I get tuple-like positional access and named fields in Rust?”

This touches on data modeling choices, not just syntax. Rust deliberately separates:

Tuples → positional, lightweight, anonymous

Structs → named, explicit, extensible

There is no built-in “named tuple” type like Python’s namedtuple or OCaml’s labeled tuples—but you can achieve equivalent (and often better) designs using a few patterns.

🧠 Design Considerations
Before jumping into syntax, think about what you actually want:

Requirement	Best Rust Construct
Named fields, readable	struct
Tuple-like compactness	tuple struct
Pattern matching + names	struct
Interop / lightweight	tuple or tuple struct
Python-style namedtuple	struct + destructuring
Rust pushes you toward clarity over brevity, so most “named tuple” use cases map cleanly to struct.

✅ Option 1 — Regular Struct (Idiomatic Rust)
This is the canonical solution.

struct Point {
    x: i32,
    y: i32,
}

let p = Point { x: 10, y: 20 };

println!("{}", p.x);
Why this is usually best
Self-documenting

Works with pattern matching

Easy to extend later

Zero runtime cost

Destructuring (tuple-like feel)
let Point { x, y } = p;
✅ Option 2 — Tuple Struct (Hybrid)
If you want tuple syntax + type name, use a tuple struct:

struct Point(i32, i32);

let p = Point(10, 20);
println!("{}", p.0);
Tradeoffs
✔ lightweight

✔ newtype-friendly

✘ no field names (.0, .1 only)

✅ Option 3 — Struct + impl Accessors (Encapsulation)
If you want both names and controlled access:

struct Point(i32, i32);

impl Point {
    fn x(&self) -> i32 { self.0 }
    fn y(&self) -> i32 { self.1 }
}

let p = Point(10, 20);
println!("{}", p.x());
This gives:

tuple storage

named API

✅ Option 4 — Pattern Matching Alias (Closest to Named Tuple Feel)
You can destructure inline to simulate named tuples:

let (x, y) = (10, 20);
println!("{x}, {y}");
Or:

let point = (10, 20);
let (x, y) = point;
This is the closest to “named tuple variables” but:

names exist only locally

not part of the type

⚙️ Option 5 — Macros / Crates (Python-style namedtuple)
There are crates that mimic named tuples:

named_tuple

tuple_struct

derive_more

Example (conceptually):

named_tuple!(Point { x: i32, y: i32 });

let p = Point { x: 1, y: 2 };
Tradeoffs
✔ concise

✘ adds dependency

✘ not idiomatic Rust

✘ less flexible than structs

🧩 Option 6 — Type Alias for Tuple (Not Truly Named)
type Point = (i32, i32);
Usage:

let p: Point = (10, 20);
But:

❌ no field names

❌ no safety improvement

🧠 Architectural Guidance
Given your broader work (compilers, VMs, IRs, etc.), here’s how to think about it:

Use struct when:
Modeling AST nodes

IR representations

VM state

Anything evolving over time

Use tuple structs when:
Newtype wrappers (e.g., Address(u32))

Performance-critical tight representations

Use tuples when:
Temporary return values

Small local transformations

⚖️ Comparison Summary
Feature	Tuple	Tuple Struct	Struct
Named fields	❌	❌	✅
Lightweight	✅	✅	✅
Readability	❌	⚠️	✅
Extensible	❌	❌	✅
Pattern matching	✅	✅	✅
🚀 Practical Recommendation
For your use cases (compilers, IR, MLPL, COR24 tooling):

Default to struct unless you have a very specific reason not to.

If you want “named tuple ergonomics,” combine:

struct

destructuring

short field names

🧪 Example (Compiler IR Style)
struct Add {
    lhs: u32,
    rhs: u32,
}

let Add { lhs, rhs } = node;
This gives you:

named semantics

tuple-like unpacking

future extensibility (e.g., add metadata)

If you want something closer to OCaml
Rust intentionally avoids:

(x=1, y=2)
But you can approximate with:

struct Point { x: i32, y: i32 }
and destructuring.

👍 Bottom line
Rust doesn’t have “named tuples” because:

struct is the named tuple—just more powerful.

If you want, I can show:

how to design IR nodes using struct vs tuple tradeoffs

or how to build a macro that generates named-tuple-like types with zero boilerplate

i will show you the signature of this verb:

⠀

⠀
now lets mint a tuple variable:
 ▪coord₂ ───‣ ⎛x y⎠ 

this tuple variable is actually a verb that pushes its own local vars onto the stack for us whenever invoked.

note that subscript "2" that suffixes the name of the tuple variable, this is to inform both compiler and reader that this verb outputs 2 values.


side note:
ALL verbs will ALWAYS have the mapping operator (this guy: "───‣") in their signature.
so macros, functions, operators, etc. always have "───‣" in their signatures.


we could also have minted and initialized the coord₂ variable in one stroke this way:
▪coord₂ ───‣ ⎛x y⎠  ⟵  3 , 9


or seperately:

coord₂ ⟵ 3 , 9


because it is a true tuple var, we can easily do this:

me thinks you get the idea.

⠀
Mike@work — 2:24 PM

marc f. — 2:25 PM

plot⎛coord₂ Red 50%⎠

⠀
we can visually see that the verb plot is having FOUR values passed to it, even though only 3 names appear in the parm list of its invocation.
and we can have as much refinement of control/expression as we please.

so:

⠀
notice that i didn't have to overload the plot function.
i just use it willy-nilly.

You want a Rust analogue of:

plot⎛coord₂ Red 50%⎠
Rust does not have automatic tuple-splicing in function calls, so this will not work directly:

plot(coord2, Color::Red, 0.5); // not the same as plot(x, y, color, alpha)
But Rust can model the idea cleanly with named tuple-like structs and a trait that converts different “point descriptions” into the canonical four plotting arguments.

Rust version: tuple variables as “argument bundles”

struct Coord2 {
    x: i32,
    y: i32,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct Coord3 {
    x: i32,
    y: i32,
    z: i32,

1	# AGENTS.md
2	
3	Instructions for AI coding agents (Claude Code, opencode, Cursor, etc.)
4	working in this repository. This project uses **agentrail** to keep
5	multi-session agent work on track. Follow these rules exactly.
6	
7	Rename or copy this file to whatever your agent reads (`AGENTS.md`,
8	`CLAUDE.md`, `.cursorrules`, etc.) — the content is the same.
9	
10	---
11	
12	## The one-paragraph summary
13	
14	This project uses agentrail to record work as a sequence of **steps** in a
15	**saga**. Each session you run does exactly one step: read the step prompt
16	with `agentrail next`, start it with `agentrail begin`, do the work, commit
17	your changes with git, and close the step with `agentrail complete`. Then
18	stop — the next step is for the next session. The `.agentrail/` directory
19	is the durable record and must be tracked in git like source code.
20	
21	## The session protocol (follow exactly)
22	
23	### 1. START — read your instructions
24	
25	```bash
26	agentrail next
27	```
28	
29	This prints the current step's prompt, relevant context files, skill
30	documents, and past successful trajectories for this task type. **Read
31	the entire output carefully.** It is the instruction for this session.
32	
33	If `agentrail next` exits with no current step, the saga is paused or
34	complete — stop and ask the user what to do. Do not invent work.
35	
36	### 2. BEGIN — transition the step
37	
38	```bash
39	agentrail begin
40	```
41	
42	This marks the step as `in-progress`. Required before doing work.
43	
44	### 3. WORK — do exactly what the step prompt says
45	
46	- Do not ask the user "want me to proceed?" or "shall I start?". The
47	  step prompt **is** your instruction. Execute it.
48	- Do not expand scope. If you notice other problems, note them for a
49	  future step — do not silently fix them in this one.
50	- Stay within the files the step prompt references. If you need to touch
51	  something outside that scope, pause and ask.
52	
53	### 4. COMMIT — commit your work with git
54	
55	```bash
56	git add <files>
57	git commit -m "<clear message>"
58	```
59	
60	**This must happen before `agentrail complete`.** `agentrail complete`
61	captures the current `HEAD` commit hash into the step's `commits` field,
62	which is how future `agentrail audit` runs link the step back to its
63	commit. If you complete before committing, the linkage is wrong.
64	
65	Include `.agentrail/` files you touched in the commit — they are part of
66	the record.
67	
68	### 5. COMPLETE — close the step
69	
70	```bash
71	agentrail complete \
72	  --summary "what you accomplished in one or two sentences" \
73	  --reward 1 \
74	  --actions "tools and approach used"
75	```
76	
77	Flags:
78	- `--reward 1` on success, `--reward -1 --failure-mode "<what-went-wrong>"`
79	  on failure. Reward is used for trajectory recording so future sessions
80	  can learn from what worked.
81	- Add `--done` if this was the last step of the saga.
82	- Use `--next-slug` and `--next-prompt` to define the next step if you
83	  know what it should be; otherwise the human will plan it.
84	
85	### 6. STOP — do not continue
86	
87	**Do not make any further changes after `agentrail complete`.** Any
88	changes after complete are untracked by the saga and invisible to the
89	next session. If you see more work to do, it belongs in the next step,
90	not this one.
91	
92	---
93	
94	## Rules for `.agentrail/` (CRITICAL — do not violate)
95	
96	The `.agentrail/` directory is the durable record of saga/step history.
97	Treat it like source code.
98	
99	### Always track it in git
100	
101	- `.agentrail/` **must** be tracked in git. Never add it to `.gitignore`.
102	  If you inherit a repo that has `.agentrail/` ignored, that is a bug —
103	  unignore it and commit the existing contents first.
104	- Commit step artifacts as each step completes, in the same commit as
105	  your code changes.
106	
107	### Never edit or delete files under `.agentrail/` by hand
108	
109	- **Do not** `rm`, `rm -rf`, `mv`, or use `Write`/`Edit` on any file
110	  under `.agentrail/` or `.agentrail-archive/`.
111	- Always go through agentrail subcommands: `init`, `add`, `begin`,
112	  `complete`, `abort`, `archive`, `plan`, `audit`.
113	- Direct deletion of untracked step files is **unrecoverable** — git
114	  reflog cannot restore blobs that were never staged. This has happened
115	  before and lost saga history.
116	
117	### Commit order matters
118	
119	Work → `git add` → `git commit` → `agentrail complete`. In that order.
120	Completing before committing means `commits` is empty and the audit
121	command can't link step to commit.
122	
123	---
124	
125	## Recovering from gaps
126	
127	If history gets out of sync — for example, an agent made commits without
128	running `agentrail complete`, or steps were added without matching
129	commits — use the audit command.
130	
131	```bash
132	agentrail audit                    # human-readable markdown report
133	agentrail audit --emit-commands    # shell script of suggested add lines
134	agentrail audit --since v1.0       # only look at commits after v1.0
135	```
136	
137	The report has four sections:
138	
139	1. **Matched** — commits that line up with a saga step (by recorded hash
140	   or by timestamp window for legacy steps).
141	2. **Orphan commits** — commits with no matching step. These are the
142	   gaps.
143	3. **Orphan steps** — steps whose recorded commit isn't in the current
144	   history (rebased away, squashed, never made).
145	4. **Working tree** — uncommitted changes. Reported for awareness, not
146	   turned into commands.
147	
148	With `--emit-commands`, the tool prints a shell script with one
149	`agentrail add --commit <hash> --slug ... --prompt ...` line per orphan
150	commit. **Review and edit the slugs and prompts before running** — the
151	defaults are seeded from commit subjects and need human judgment.
152	
153	## Retroactive history for old projects
154	
155	If the project predates agentrail and you want to add a saga on top of
156	existing history:
157	
158	```bash
159	agentrail audit --emit-commands > rebuild.sh
160	# Edit rebuild.sh: reword slugs and prompts as coherent step descriptions
161	sh rebuild.sh
162	```
163	
164	The script begins with `agentrail init --retroactive --name development`
165	(when no saga exists) and then adds one step per historical commit.
166	Retroactive sagas are marked in `saga.toml` so future audits know those
167	commits are claimed.
168	
169	Going forward from there, run new sagas normally.
170	
171	## Safety net: `agentrail snapshot`
172	
173	If you have files under `.agentrail/` that are not yet committed and
174	you're about to do something risky (a big agent run, a rebase, cleaning
175	up untracked files), run:
176	
177	```bash
178	agentrail snapshot
179	```
180	
181	This creates a git commit under `refs/agentrail/snapshots/<timestamp>`
182	containing a copy of `.agentrail/` and `.agentrail-archive/`. The user's
183	real git index is not touched — it uses a throwaway temp index under the
184	hood. The snapshot survives `git gc` because a named ref holds it.
185	
186	Restore from a snapshot with a normal git command:
187	
188	```bash
189	git restore --source=refs/agentrail/snapshots/<timestamp> \
190	    -- .agentrail .agentrail-archive
191	```
192	
193	List existing snapshots with `agentrail snapshot --list`.
194	
195	This is a safety net, not a replacement for committing. Commit your
196	work normally — use snapshot only as belt-and-suspenders insurance.
197	
198	---
199	
200	## Quick reference
201	
202	| Command | When to use |
203	|---|---|
204	| `agentrail next` | Start of every session |
205	| `agentrail begin` | After reading `next`, before working |
206	| `agentrail complete --summary "..." --reward 1` | After committing |
207	| `agentrail status` | Inspect current saga state (read-only) |
208	| `agentrail history` | Show all step summaries (read-only) |
209	| `agentrail plan --update ...` | Revise the saga plan |
210	| `agentrail add --slug ... --prompt ...` | Add a step without completing current one (maintenance mode) |
211	| `agentrail abort --reason "..."` | Mark current step as blocked |
212	| `agentrail archive --reason "..."` | Close out a saga and start fresh |
213	| `agentrail audit` | Diagnose saga-vs-git gaps |
214	| `agentrail snapshot` | Save a safety-net copy of `.agentrail/` into the git object store (opt-in) |
215	| `agentrail snapshot --list` | List existing snapshot refs |
216	
217	## What not to do
218	
219	- Do not run `agentrail complete` before committing.
220	- Do not touch files under `.agentrail/` with anything other than
221	  `agentrail` subcommands.
222	- Do not keep working after `agentrail complete`. Stop and let the next
223	  session pick up.
224	- Do not add `.agentrail/` to `.gitignore`.
225	- Do not skip `agentrail next` "because you remember what the step was"
226	  — the next output includes trajectories and skill docs that change as
227	  the system learns.

# AGENTS.md

Instructions for AI coding agents (Claude Code, opencode, Cursor, etc.)
working in this repository. This project uses **agentrail** to keep
multi-session agent work on track. Follow these rules exactly.

Rename or copy this file to whatever your agent reads (`AGENTS.md`,
`CLAUDE.md`, `.cursorrules`, etc.) — the content is the same.

---

## The one-paragraph summary

This project uses agentrail to record work as a sequence of **steps** in a
**saga**. Each session you run does exactly one step: read the step prompt
with `agentrail next`, start it with `agentrail begin`, do the work, commit
your changes with git, and close the step with `agentrail complete`. Then
stop — the next step is for the next session. The `.agentrail/` directory
is the durable record and must be tracked in git like source code.

## The session protocol (follow exactly)

### 1. START — read your instructions

```bash
agentrail next
```

This prints the current step's prompt, relevant context files, skill
documents, and past successful trajectories for this task type. **Read
the entire output carefully.** It is the instruction for this session.

If `agentrail next` exits with no current step, the saga is paused or
complete — stop and ask the user what to do. Do not invent work.

### 2. BEGIN — transition the step

```bash
agentrail begin
```

This marks the step as `in-progress`. Required before doing work.

### 3. WORK — do exactly what the step prompt says

- Do not ask the user "want me to proceed?" or "shall I start?". The
  step prompt **is** your instruction. Execute it.
- Do not expand scope. If you notice other problems, note them for a
  future step — do not silently fix them in this one.
- Stay within the files the step prompt references. If you need to touch
  something outside that scope, pause and ask.

### 4. COMMIT — commit your work with git

```bash
git add <files>
git commit -m "<clear message>"
```

**This must happen before `agentrail complete`.** `agentrail complete`
captures the current `HEAD` commit hash into the step's `commits` field,
which is how future `agentrail audit` runs link the step back to its
commit. If you complete before committing, the linkage is wrong.

Include `.agentrail/` files you touched in the commit — they are part of
the record.

### 5. COMPLETE — close the step

```bash
agentrail complete \
  --summary "what you accomplished in one or two sentences" \
  --reward 1 \
  --actions "tools and approach used"
```

Flags:
- `--reward 1` on success, `--reward -1 --failure-mode "<what-went-wrong>"`
  on failure. Reward is used for trajectory recording so future sessions
  can learn from what worked.
- Add `--done` if this was the last step of the saga.
- Use `--next-slug` and `--next-prompt` to define the next step if you
  know what it should be; otherwise the human will plan it.

### 6. STOP — do not continue

**Do not make any further changes after `agentrail complete`.** Any
changes after complete are untracked by the saga and invisible to the
next session. If you see more work to do, it belongs in the next step,
not this one.

---

## Rules for `.agentrail/` (CRITICAL — do not violate)

The `.agentrail/` directory is the durable record of saga/step history.
Treat it like source code.

### Always track it in git

- `.agentrail/` **must** be tracked in git. Never add it to `.gitignore`.
  If you inherit a repo that has `.agentrail/` ignored, that is a bug —
  unignore it and commit the existing contents first.
- Commit step artifacts as each step completes, in the same commit as
  your code changes.

### Never edit or delete files under `.agentrail/` by hand

- **Do not** `rm`, `rm -rf`, `mv`, or use `Write`/`Edit` on any file
  under `.agentrail/` or `.agentrail-archive/`.
- Always go through agentrail subcommands: `init`, `add`, `begin`,
  `complete`, `abort`, `archive`, `plan`, `audit`.
- Direct deletion of untracked step files is **unrecoverable** — git
  reflog cannot restore blobs that were never staged. This has happened
  before and lost saga history.

### Commit order matters

Work → `git add` → `git commit` → `agentrail complete`. In that order.
Completing before committing means `commits` is empty and the audit
command can't link step to commit.

---

## Recovering from gaps

If history gets out of sync — for example, an agent made commits without
running `agentrail complete`, or steps were added without matching
commits — use the audit command.

```bash
agentrail audit                    # human-readable markdown report
agentrail audit --emit-commands    # shell script of suggested add lines
agentrail audit --since v1.0       # only look at commits after v1.0
```

The report has four sections:

1. **Matched** — commits that line up with a saga step (by recorded hash
   or by timestamp window for legacy steps).
2. **Orphan commits** — commits with no matching step. These are the
   gaps.
3. **Orphan steps** — steps whose recorded commit isn't in the current
   history (rebased away, squashed, never made).
4. **Working tree** — uncommitted changes. Reported for awareness, not
   turned into commands.

With `--emit-commands`, the tool prints a shell script with one
`agentrail add --commit <hash> --slug ... --prompt ...` line per orphan
commit. **Review and edit the slugs and prompts before running** — the
defaults are seeded from commit subjects and need human judgment.

## Retroactive history for old projects

If the project predates agentrail and you want to add a saga on top of
existing history:

```bash
agentrail audit --emit-commands > rebuild.sh
# Edit rebuild.sh: reword slugs and prompts as coherent step descriptions
sh rebuild.sh
```

The script begins with `agentrail init --retroactive --name development`
(when no saga exists) and then adds one step per historical commit.
Retroactive sagas are marked in `saga.toml` so future audits know those
commits are claimed.

Going forward from there, run new sagas normally.

## Safety net: `agentrail snapshot`

If you have files under `.agentrail/` that are not yet committed and
you're about to do something risky (a big agent run, a rebase, cleaning
up untracked files), run:

```bash
agentrail snapshot
```

This creates a git commit under `refs/agentrail/snapshots/<timestamp>`
containing a copy of `.agentrail/` and `.agentrail-archive/`. The user's
real git index is not touched — it uses a throwaway temp index under the
hood. The snapshot survives `git gc` because a named ref holds it.

Restore from a snapshot with a normal git command:

```bash
git restore --source=refs/agentrail/snapshots/<timestamp> \
    -- .agentrail .agentrail-archive
```

List existing snapshots with `agentrail snapshot --list`.

This is a safety net, not a replacement for committing. Commit your
work normally — use snapshot only as belt-and-suspenders insurance.

---

## Quick reference

| Command | When to use |
|---|---|
| `agentrail next` | Start of every session |
| `agentrail begin` | After reading `next`, before working |
| `agentrail complete --summary "..." --reward 1` | After committing |
| `agentrail status` | Inspect current saga state (read-only) |
| `agentrail history` | Show all step summaries (read-only) |
| `agentrail plan --update ...` | Revise the saga plan |
| `agentrail add --slug ... --prompt ...` | Add a step without completing current one (maintenance mode) |
| `agentrail abort --reason "..."` | Mark current step as blocked |
| `agentrail archive --reason "..."` | Close out a saga and start fresh |
| `agentrail audit` | Diagnose saga-vs-git gaps |
| `agentrail snapshot` | Save a safety-net copy of `.agentrail/` into the git object store (opt-in) |
| `agentrail snapshot --list` | List existing snapshot refs |

## What not to do

- Do not run `agentrail complete` before committing.
- Do not touch files under `.agentrail/` with anything other than
  `agentrail` subcommands.
- Do not keep working after `agentrail complete`. Stop and let the next
  session pick up.
- Do not add `.agentrail/` to `.gitignore`.
- Do not skip `agentrail next` "because you remember what the step was"
  — the next output includes trajectories and skill docs that change as
  the system learns.

300	    z: i32,
301	}
302	
303	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
304	enum Color {
305	    Red,
306	    Green,
307	    Blue,
308	}
309	
310	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
311	struct Transparency(f32);
312	
313	impl Transparency {
314	    fn percent(value: f32) -> Self {
315	        Self(value / 100.0)
316	    }
317	}
318	
319	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
320	struct Point4 {
321	    x: i32,
322	    y: i32,
323	    color: Color,
324	    transparency: Transparency,
325	}
326	Now define a trait meaning:
327	
328	“This thing can be expanded into the arguments needed by plot.”
329	
330	trait IntoPlotArgs {
331	    fn into_plot_args(self) -> Point4;
332	}
333	Implement it for a full point:
334	
335	impl IntoPlotArgs for Point4 {
336	    fn into_plot_args(self) -> Point4 {
337	        self
338	    }
339	}
340	And for a coordinate plus extra values:
341	
342	impl IntoPlotArgs for (Coord2, Color, Transparency) {
343	    fn into_plot_args(self) -> Point4 {
344	        let (coord, color, transparency) = self;
345	
346	        Point4 {
347	            x: coord.x,
348	            y: coord.y,
349	            color,
350	            transparency,
351	        }
352	    }
353	}
354	Now plot accepts anything that can become plotting arguments:
355	
356	fn plot(args: impl IntoPlotArgs) -> bool {
357	    let Point4 {
358	        x,
359	        y,
360	        color,
361	        transparency,
362	    } = args.into_plot_args();
363	
364	    println!(
365	        "plot x={x}, y={y}, color={color:?}, transparency={}",
366	        transparency.0
367	    );
368	
369	    true
370	}
371	Usage:
372	
373	fn main() {
374	    let coord2 = Coord2 { x: 3, y: 9 };
375	
376	    // Equivalent to: plot⎛coord₂ Red 50%⎠
377	    let success = plot((coord2, Color::Red, Transparency::percent(50.0)));
378	
379	    println!("success = {success}");
380	
381	    // Equivalent to: point₄ ───‣ ⎛x y color transparency⎠
382	    let point4 = Point4 {
383	        x: 3,
384	        y: 9,
385	        color: Color::Blue,
386	        transparency: Transparency::percent(25.0),
387	    };
388	
389	    // Equivalent to: plot⎛point₄⎠
390	    let success = plot(point4);
391	
392	    println!("success = {success}");
393	}
394	Important Rust distinction
395	In the original language idea, this:
396	
397	plot⎛coord₂ Red 50%⎠
398	means coord₂ contributes two stack values.
399	
400	In Rust, values do not automatically expand into multiple arguments. Instead, we usually make the grouping explicit:
401	
402	plot((coord2, Color::Red, Transparency::percent(50.0)));
403	That outer tuple is the “argument bundle.”
404	
405	More direct positional version
406	You can also keep the canonical low-level function separate:
407	
408	fn plot_raw(x: i32, y: i32, color: Color, transparency: Transparency) -> bool {
409	    println!("plot x={x}, y={y}, color={color:?}, transparency={}", transparency.0);
410	    true
411	}
412	Then use a wrapper trait:
413	
414	fn plot(args: impl IntoPlotArgs) -> bool {
415	    let p = args.into_plot_args();
416	    plot_raw(p.x, p.y, p.color, p.transparency)
417	}
418	That maps nicely to your conceptual model:
419	
420	coord₂ ───‣ x y
421	point₄ ───‣ x y color transparency
422	plot consumes x y color transparency
423	For coord₃
424	A 3D coordinate can be modeled separately:
425	
426	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
427	struct Coord3 {
428	    x: i32,
429	    y: i32,
430	    z: i32,
431	}
432	Then either:
433	
434	fn plot3d(coord: Coord3, color: Color, transparency: Transparency) -> bool {
435	    println!(
436	        "plot3d x={}, y={}, z={}, color={:?}, transparency={}",
437	        coord.x, coord.y, coord.z, color, transparency.0
438	    );
439	
440	    true
441	}
442	Or create another trait:
443	
444	trait IntoPlot3dArgs {
445	    fn into_plot3d_args(self) -> (Coord3, Color, Transparency);
446	}
447	Bottom line
448	The Rust equivalent of your tuple-verb idea is usually:
449	
450	struct Coord2 { x: i32, y: i32 }
451	struct Point4 { x: i32, y: i32, color: Color, transparency: Transparency }
452	
453	trait IntoPlotArgs {
454	    fn into_plot_args(self) -> Point4;
455	}
456	
457	fn plot(args: impl IntoPlotArgs) -> bool { ... }
458	This gives you most of the expressive benefit:
459	
460	plot((coord2, Color::Red, Transparency::percent(50.0)));
461	plot(point4);
462	without overloading plot, and while staying idiomatic Rust.
463	
464	
465	How can you take this discussion and produce equivalent APL?
466	watch this:
467	instead of functions, for us, the verbs for obtaining the max and min are binary operators;
468	these guys ───‣  ⋏ and  ⋎
469	
470	so:    a ⋏ b    would be   max(a, b)  in other PLs.
471	but, we can overload for the outputs, and do this:
472	⠀
473	q , r  ⟵  a ⋏₂ b
474	⠀
475	
476	which would guarantee that q be bigger or same size as r.
477	we can sort a and b this way:
478	⠀
479	a , b  ⟵  a ⋎₂ b
480	⠀
481	
482	so that a is guaranteed to now be smaller (or same size) as b
483	so we can have verbs that return a single value, or a tuple of values.
484	Mike@work — 2:36 PM
485	3 ⌈ 5 in APL (going back to your max) 
486	marc f. — 2:37 PM
487	⠀
488	quotient ⟵ a ÷ b
489	
490	integer , fractional  ⟵  a ÷₂ b
491	⠀
492	
493	
494	see what i mean Vern?
495	
496	Thought for a couple of seconds
497	You can model this in APL, but with one important caveat:
498	
499	APL functions return one array value, but that value may itself be a vector, nested vector, matrix, namespace, etc.
500	
501	So “multiple return values” are usually represented as a single structured result that you destructure by assignment.
502	
503	Max / min in APL
504	APL already has scalar max and min:
505	
506	3 ⌈ 5   ⍝ max → 5
507	3 ⌊ 5   ⍝ min → 3
508	So your notation:
509	
510	a ⋏ b      ⟹ max(a,b)
511	a ⋎ b      ⟹ min(a,b)
512	maps naturally to:
513	
514	a ⌈ b
515	a ⌊ b
516	Returning both values: “sort two values”
517	Your:
518	
519	q , r ⟵ a ⋏₂ b
520	meaning:
521	
522	q ≥ r
523	can be written as an APL function returning a two-element vector:
524	
525	Max2 ← { (⍺⌈⍵) (⍺⌊⍵) }
526	Usage:
527	
528	q r ← 3 Max2 5
529	q
530	⍝ 5
531	
532	r
533	⍝ 3
534	Similarly, your:
535	
536	a , b ⟵ a ⋎₂ b
537	meaning:
538	
539	a ≤ b
540	can be:
541	
542	Min2 ← { (⍺⌊⍵) (⍺⌈⍵) }
543	Usage:
544	
545	a b ← 3 Min2 5
546	a
547	⍝ 3
548	
549	b
550	⍝ 5
551	Quotient vs quotient-and-remainder style
552	APL already has division:
553	
554	quotient ← a ÷ b
555	For integer and fractional parts:
556	
557	Div2 ← {
558	    q ← ⌊⍺÷⍵
559	    f ← (⍺÷⍵) - q
560	    q f
561	}
562	Usage:
563	
564	integer fractional ← 7 Div2 3
565	integer
566	⍝ 2
567	
568	fractional
569	⍝ 0.3333333333
570	But if you mean integer quotient and remainder, then this is closer:
571	
572	DivRem ← {
573	    q ← ⌊⍺÷⍵
574	    r ← ⍺ - ⍵×q
575	    q r
576	}
577	Usage:
578	
579	q r ← 7 DivRem 3
580	q
581	⍝ 2
582	
583	r
584	⍝ 1
585	The APL idea
586	So this language idea:
587	
588	q , r ⟵ a ⋏₂ b
589	a , b ⟵ a ⋎₂ b
590	integer , fractional ⟵ a ÷₂ b
591	maps to idiomatic APL as:
592	
593	q r ← a Max2 b
594	a b ← a Min2 b
595	integer fractional ← a Div2 b
596	Compact version
597	Max2   ← { (⍺⌈⍵) (⍺⌊⍵) }
598	Min2   ← { (⍺⌊⍵) (⍺⌈⍵) }
599	Div2   ← { q←⌊⍺÷⍵ ⋄ q ((⍺÷⍵)-q) }
600	DivRem ← { q←⌊⍺÷⍵ ⋄ q (⍺-⍵×q) }
601	The closest APL equivalent to “a verb that returns a tuple” is:
602	
603	result ← function arguments
604	x y z ← result
605	or directly:
606	
607	x y z ← function arguments
608	APL does not need special suffix syntax like ⋏₂; you can just name the variant Max2, or in an APL-like language, define ⌈₂ as a distinct glyph/operator meaning “return both max and min.”
609	
610	
611	Does APL2 make this easier, more concise?
612	
613	Short answer: APL2 doesn’t change the core model, but it does make this style cleaner and more expressive thanks to nested arrays and strand assignment.
614	
615	🧠 Restating the goal
616	You want this conceptual pattern:
617	
618	q , r ⟵ a ⋏₂ b      ⍝ return (max, min)
619	a , b ⟵ a ⋎₂ b      ⍝ return (min, max)
620	integer , fractional ⟵ a ÷₂ b
621	i.e., verbs that can return multiple values (tuples) and destructure them naturally.
622	
623	🧩 What APL2 adds
624	Compared to classic APL:
625	
626	1. First-class nested arrays
627	You can return structured results cleanly.
628	
629	2. Strand assignment
630	You can destructure directly:
631	
632	q r ← result
633	3. Cleaner composition (no hacks needed)
634	✅ APL2 version (clean and concise)
635	Max + Min together
636	Max2 ← { (⍺⌈⍵) (⍺⌊⍵) }

740	A programming language designer claims that no programming language has named tuples.  What does this mean?
741	
742	Let’s unpack the claim carefully, because it’s not literally true, but it can be true depending on how the designer is defining terms.
743	
744	🧠 Restating the claim
745	“No programming language has named tuples.”
746	
747	This usually means:
748	
749	What people call “named tuples” are actually something else (typically structs/records), not true tuples with names.
750	
751	So the argument hinges on what counts as a tuple.
752	
753	🧩 Step 1 — What is a “tuple” (strict sense)?
754	In a strict, type-theoretic / PL design sense:
755	
756	A tuple is:
757	
758	Ordered
759	
760	Positional
761	
762	Anonymous field access
763	
764	Identified by arity and types only
765	
766	Example (Rust / many languages):
767	
768	let t: (i32, i32) = (3, 9);
769	Access:
770	
771	t.0
772	t.1
773	Key property:
774	
775	The meaning of each element is determined by position, not name.
776	
777	🧩 Step 2 — What is a “named tuple”?
778	In many languages (Python, C#, Swift, etc.), a “named tuple” looks like:
779	
780	Point(x=3, y=9)
781	or:
782	
783	(int x, int y) p = (3, 9);
784	These give you:
785	
786	Positional structure
787	
788	Named field access (p.x, p.y)
789	
790	🧨 The designer’s argument
791	The claim is usually:
792	
793	The moment you give tuple elements names, it is no longer a tuple — it becomes a record/struct.
794	
795	Because:
796	
797	1. Names change identity semantics
798	Compare:
799	
800	(i32, i32)
801	vs:
802	
803	{x: i32, y: i32}
804	In many type systems:
805	
806	(i32, i32) ≠ (y: i32, x: i32) (if names matter)
807	
808	But {x: i32, y: i32} = {y: i32, x: i32} (order doesn’t matter)
809	
810	So:
811	
812	Named fields imply record semantics, not tuple semantics.
813	
814	2. Access mode changes
815	Tuple:
816	
817	t.0
818	Named tuple:
819	
820	p.x
821	That’s no longer positional—it’s field-based access.
822	
823	3. Evolution behavior differs
824	Tuples:
825	
826	Fragile to change (add/remove/reorder breaks everything)
827	
828	Records:
829	
830	Can evolve (add fields, reorder, defaults, etc.)
831	
832	So:
833	
834	Named tuples behave like lightweight records, not tuples.
835	
836	⚖️ So what languages call “named tuples”?
837	Python — collections.namedtuple
838	Point = namedtuple('Point', ['x', 'y'])
839	Internally:
840	
841	It's basically a tuple subclass + attribute access
842	
843	But semantically:
844	
845	Acts like a record
846	
847	C#
848	(int x, int y) p = (3, 9);
849	Names:
850	
851	Are mostly compile-time sugar
852	
853	Can be erased or ignored
854	
855	So:
856	
857	It’s still fundamentally a tuple
858	
859	Names are optional metadata
860	
861	Rust
862	No named tuples.
863	
864	You must use:
865	
866	struct Point { x: i32, y: i32 }
867	Rust’s stance is effectively:
868	
869	If you want names, use a struct.
870	
871	Swift
872	let p = (x: 3, y: 9)
873	But:
874	
875	Type identity is still positional
876	
877	Names are partially cosmetic
878	
879	🧠 The deeper philosophical point
880	The designer is likely asserting:
881	
882	There are only two real categories:
883	
884	Tuples → positional, anonymous
885	
886	Records/structs → named, semantic
887	
888	And “named tuples” are:
889	
890	either
891	
892	tuples with cosmetic labels
893	
894	or records pretending to be tuples
895	
896	🧩 Mapping to your earlier language ideas
897	Your system:
898	
899	⎛x y⎠
900	is interesting because:
901	
902	It is a tuple
903	
904	But you’re associating names with positions
905	
906	And allowing destructuring:
907	
908	a , b ⟵ coord₂
909	So your design is closer to:
910	
911	tuples with semantic labels that don’t change positional identity
912	
913	That’s actually rare.
914	
915	⚖️ Summary
916	The claim:
917	
918	“No programming language has named tuples”
919	
920	means:
921	
922	In a strict sense, you must choose:
923	
924	positional tuples
925	
926	or named records
927	
928	but not both simultaneously without changing semantics.
929	
930	👍 Practical takeaway
931	Most languages that “have named tuples” are:
932	
933	either adding labels to tuples (cosmetic)
934	
935	or providing lightweight structs
936	
937	Truly combining:
938	
939	positional semantics
940	
941	named access
942	
943	interchangeable usage
944	
945	is non-trivial and uncommon
946	
947	🚀 Your design insight (important)
948	What you’re exploring:
949	
950	coord₂ ───‣ ⎛x y⎠
951	plot⎛coord₂ Red 50%⎠
952	is actually pushing toward:
953	
954	first-class tuple decomposition + argument splicing + semantic labeling
955	
956	That goes beyond what most mainstream languages support.
957	
958	If you want, I can:
959	
960	classify languages by how “real” their named tuples are
961	
962	or show how to design a type system that truly supports your model without ambiguity
963	
964	
965	So this can be added to which languages and how. e.g. Rust via traits, possibly macros; lisp via macros, Forth via words (perhaps), others?  How would you add what is missing to "popular" languages/
966	
967	Thought for a few seconds
968	You’re asking how to add the missing pieces:
969	
970	named tuple bundles
971	
972	tuple destructuring
973	
974	argument expansion / splicing
975	
976	verbs that accept either raw args or bundled args
977	
978	optionally, arity-signaling names like coord₂, point₄, ÷₂
979	
980	Most popular languages already have some of this. The hard part is combining all of it cleanly.
981	
982	Core pattern
983	Your model can be described as:
984	
985	coord₂ ───‣ x y
986	plot   ⟵  x y color transparency ───‣ successˀ
987	
988	plot(coord₂ Red 50%)
989	Meaning:
990	
991	coord₂ expands into two arguments at the call site.
992	Most languages lack that exact automatic expansion. They usually require either:
993	
994	plot(coord.x, coord.y, red, alpha)
995	or:
996	
997	plot(coord, red, alpha)
998	or:
999

}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
enum Color {
    Red,
    Green,
    Blue,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct Transparency(f32);

impl Transparency {
    fn percent(value: f32) -> Self {
        Self(value / 100.0)
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct Point4 {
    x: i32,
    y: i32,
    color: Color,
    transparency: Transparency,
}
Now define a trait meaning:

“This thing can be expanded into the arguments needed by plot.”

trait IntoPlotArgs {
    fn into_plot_args(self) -> Point4;
}
Implement it for a full point:

impl IntoPlotArgs for Point4 {
    fn into_plot_args(self) -> Point4 {
        self
    }
}
And for a coordinate plus extra values:

impl IntoPlotArgs for (Coord2, Color, Transparency) {
    fn into_plot_args(self) -> Point4 {
        let (coord, color, transparency) = self;

        Point4 {
            x: coord.x,
            y: coord.y,
            color,
            transparency,
        }
    }
}
Now plot accepts anything that can become plotting arguments:

fn plot(args: impl IntoPlotArgs) -> bool {
    let Point4 {
        x,
        y,
        color,
        transparency,
    } = args.into_plot_args();

    println!(
        "plot x={x}, y={y}, color={color:?}, transparency={}",
        transparency.0
    );

    true
}
Usage:

fn main() {
    let coord2 = Coord2 { x: 3, y: 9 };

    // Equivalent to: plot⎛coord₂ Red 50%⎠
    let success = plot((coord2, Color::Red, Transparency::percent(50.0)));

    println!("success = {success}");

    // Equivalent to: point₄ ───‣ ⎛x y color transparency⎠
    let point4 = Point4 {
        x: 3,
        y: 9,
        color: Color::Blue,
        transparency: Transparency::percent(25.0),
    };

    // Equivalent to: plot⎛point₄⎠
    let success = plot(point4);

    println!("success = {success}");
}
Important Rust distinction
In the original language idea, this:

plot⎛coord₂ Red 50%⎠
means coord₂ contributes two stack values.

In Rust, values do not automatically expand into multiple arguments. Instead, we usually make the grouping explicit:

plot((coord2, Color::Red, Transparency::percent(50.0)));
That outer tuple is the “argument bundle.”

More direct positional version
You can also keep the canonical low-level function separate:

fn plot_raw(x: i32, y: i32, color: Color, transparency: Transparency) -> bool {
    println!("plot x={x}, y={y}, color={color:?}, transparency={}", transparency.0);
    true
}
Then use a wrapper trait:

fn plot(args: impl IntoPlotArgs) -> bool {
    let p = args.into_plot_args();
    plot_raw(p.x, p.y, p.color, p.transparency)
}
That maps nicely to your conceptual model:

coord₂ ───‣ x y
point₄ ───‣ x y color transparency
plot consumes x y color transparency
For coord₃
A 3D coordinate can be modeled separately:

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct Coord3 {
    x: i32,
    y: i32,
    z: i32,
}
Then either:

fn plot3d(coord: Coord3, color: Color, transparency: Transparency) -> bool {
    println!(
        "plot3d x={}, y={}, z={}, color={:?}, transparency={}",
        coord.x, coord.y, coord.z, color, transparency.0
    );

    true
}
Or create another trait:

trait IntoPlot3dArgs {
    fn into_plot3d_args(self) -> (Coord3, Color, Transparency);
}
Bottom line
The Rust equivalent of your tuple-verb idea is usually:

struct Coord2 { x: i32, y: i32 }
struct Point4 { x: i32, y: i32, color: Color, transparency: Transparency }

trait IntoPlotArgs {
    fn into_plot_args(self) -> Point4;
}

fn plot(args: impl IntoPlotArgs) -> bool { ... }
This gives you most of the expressive benefit:

plot((coord2, Color::Red, Transparency::percent(50.0)));
plot(point4);
without overloading plot, and while staying idiomatic Rust.


How can you take this discussion and produce equivalent APL?
watch this:
instead of functions, for us, the verbs for obtaining the max and min are binary operators;
these guys ───‣  ⋏ and  ⋎

so:    a ⋏ b    would be   max(a, b)  in other PLs.
but, we can overload for the outputs, and do this:
⠀
q , r  ⟵  a ⋏₂ b
⠀

which would guarantee that q be bigger or same size as r.
we can sort a and b this way:
⠀
a , b  ⟵  a ⋎₂ b
⠀

so that a is guaranteed to now be smaller (or same size) as b
so we can have verbs that return a single value, or a tuple of values.

see what i mean Vern?

Thought for a couple of seconds
You can model this in APL, but with one important caveat:

APL functions return one array value, but that value may itself be a vector, nested vector, matrix, namespace, etc.

So “multiple return values” are usually represented as a single structured result that you destructure by assignment.

Max / min in APL
APL already has scalar max and min:

3 ⌈ 5   ⍝ max → 5
3 ⌊ 5   ⍝ min → 3
So your notation:

a ⋏ b      ⟹ max(a,b)
a ⋎ b      ⟹ min(a,b)
maps naturally to:

a ⌈ b
a ⌊ b
Returning both values: “sort two values”
Your:

q , r ⟵ a ⋏₂ b
meaning:

q ≥ r
can be written as an APL function returning a two-element vector:

Max2 ← { (⍺⌈⍵) (⍺⌊⍵) }
Usage:

q r ← 3 Max2 5
q
⍝ 5

r
⍝ 3
Similarly, your:

a , b ⟵ a ⋎₂ b
meaning:

a ≤ b
can be:

Min2 ← { (⍺⌊⍵) (⍺⌈⍵) }
Usage:

a b ← 3 Min2 5
a
⍝ 3

b
⍝ 5
Quotient vs quotient-and-remainder style
APL already has division:

quotient ← a ÷ b
For integer and fractional parts:

Div2 ← {
    q ← ⌊⍺÷⍵
    f ← (⍺÷⍵) - q
    q f
}
Usage:

integer fractional ← 7 Div2 3
integer
⍝ 2

fractional
⍝ 0.3333333333
But if you mean integer quotient and remainder, then this is closer:

DivRem ← {
    q ← ⌊⍺÷⍵
    r ← ⍺ - ⍵×q
    q r
}
Usage:

q r ← 7 DivRem 3
q
⍝ 2

r
⍝ 1
The APL idea
So this language idea:

q , r ⟵ a ⋏₂ b
a , b ⟵ a ⋎₂ b
integer , fractional ⟵ a ÷₂ b
maps to idiomatic APL as:

q r ← a Max2 b
a b ← a Min2 b
integer fractional ← a Div2 b
Compact version
Max2   ← { (⍺⌈⍵) (⍺⌊⍵) }
Min2   ← { (⍺⌊⍵) (⍺⌈⍵) }
Div2   ← { q←⌊⍺÷⍵ ⋄ q ((⍺÷⍵)-q) }
DivRem ← { q←⌊⍺÷⍵ ⋄ q (⍺-⍵×q) }
The closest APL equivalent to “a verb that returns a tuple” is:

result ← function arguments
x y z ← result
or directly:

x y z ← function arguments
APL does not need special suffix syntax like ⋏₂; you can just name the variant Max2, or in an APL-like language, define ⌈₂ as a distinct glyph/operator meaning “return both max and min.”


Does APL2 make this easier, more concise?

Short answer: APL2 doesn’t change the core model, but it does make this style cleaner and more expressive thanks to nested arrays and strand assignment.

🧠 Restating the goal
You want this conceptual pattern:

q , r ⟵ a ⋏₂ b      ⍝ return (max, min)
a , b ⟵ a ⋎₂ b      ⍝ return (min, max)
integer , fractional ⟵ a ÷₂ b
i.e., verbs that can return multiple values (tuples) and destructure them naturally.

🧩 What APL2 adds
Compared to classic APL:

1. First-class nested arrays
You can return structured results cleanly.

2. Strand assignment
You can destructure directly:

q r ← result
3. Cleaner composition (no hacks needed)
✅ APL2 version (clean and concise)
Max + Min together
Max2 ← { (⍺⌈⍵) (⍺⌊⍵) }
Usage:

q r ← 3 Max2 5
That’s already quite close to your idea.

Even more APL-ish: avoid recomputing
Max2 ← {
    hi ← ⍺⌈⍵
    lo ← ⍺⌊⍵
    hi lo
}
Sorting two values
Sort2 ← { (⍺⌊⍵) (⍺⌈⍵) }

a b ← 5 Sort2 3
⍝ a=3, b=5
🔢 Division variants
Integer + fractional
Div2 ← {
    q ← ⌊⍺÷⍵
    f ← (⍺÷⍵) - q
    q f
}
i f ← 7 Div2 3
Quotient + remainder (more common)
DivRem ← {
    q ← ⌊⍺÷⍵
    r ← ⍺ - ⍵×q
    q r
}
q r ← 7 DivRem 3
✨ Making it feel like your “⋏₂” idea
APL2 doesn’t support glyph overloading like ⋏₂, but you can get close:

Naming convention
⌈2 ← { (⍺⌈⍵) (⍺⌊⍵) }
⌊2 ← { (⍺⌊⍵) (⍺⌈⍵) }
Usage:

q r ← 3 ⌈2 5
a b ← 3 ⌊2 5
This is very close to your notation:

a ⋏₂ b
a ⋎₂ b
🧠 Why this works in APL2
APL2 treats:

(⍺⌈⍵) (⍺⌊⍵)
as a vector (strand) of results, which is exactly your “tuple.”

Then:

q r ← ...
is parallel assignment (destructuring).

⚖️ Comparison to your model
Concept	Your language	APL2
multi-return verb	⋏₂	function returning vector
tuple	⎛x y⎠	strand / vector
destructuring	q , r ⟵	q r ←
overload arity	suffix _2	naming convention
🚀 Idiomatic APL2 takeaway
APL2 already naturally supports your idea:

Max2 ← { (⍺⌈⍵) (⍺⌊⍵) }
q r ← a Max2 b
No special syntax needed.

💡 Deeper insight (important)
What you’re designing:

verbs that return multiple values

In APL thinking becomes:

verbs return arrays; arrays can represent multiple values

That’s why APL doesn’t need special tuple-return syntax.

👍 Final answer
Yes — APL2 makes this easier and more natural, not by adding tuple-return syntax, but because:

Nested arrays act as tuples

Strand assignment gives clean destructuring

Functions returning vectors are idiomatic

wrapped in a function.

A programming language designer claims that no programming language has named tuples.  What does this mean?

Let’s unpack the claim carefully, because it’s not literally true, but it can be true depending on how the designer is defining terms.

🧠 Restating the claim
“No programming language has named tuples.”

This usually means:

What people call “named tuples” are actually something else (typically structs/records), not true tuples with names.

So the argument hinges on what counts as a tuple.

🧩 Step 1 — What is a “tuple” (strict sense)?
In a strict, type-theoretic / PL design sense:

A tuple is:

Ordered

Positional

Anonymous field access

Identified by arity and types only

Example (Rust / many languages):

let t: (i32, i32) = (3, 9);
Access:

t.0
t.1
Key property:

The meaning of each element is determined by position, not name.

🧩 Step 2 — What is a “named tuple”?
In many languages (Python, C#, Swift, etc.), a “named tuple” looks like:

Point(x=3, y=9)
or:

(int x, int y) p = (3, 9);
These give you:

Positional structure

Named field access (p.x, p.y)

🧨 The designer’s argument
The claim is usually:

The moment you give tuple elements names, it is no longer a tuple — it becomes a record/struct.

Because:

1. Names change identity semantics
Compare:

(i32, i32)
vs:

{x: i32, y: i32}
In many type systems:

(i32, i32) ≠ (y: i32, x: i32) (if names matter)

But {x: i32, y: i32} = {y: i32, x: i32} (order doesn’t matter)

So:

Named fields imply record semantics, not tuple semantics.

2. Access mode changes
Tuple:

t.0
Named tuple:

p.x
That’s no longer positional—it’s field-based access.

3. Evolution behavior differs
Tuples:

Fragile to change (add/remove/reorder breaks everything)

Records:

Can evolve (add fields, reorder, defaults, etc.)

So:

Named tuples behave like lightweight records, not tuples.

⚖️ So what languages call “named tuples”?
Python — collections.namedtuple
Point = namedtuple('Point', ['x', 'y'])
Internally:

It's basically a tuple subclass + attribute access

But semantically:

Acts like a record

C#
(int x, int y) p = (3, 9);
Names:

Are mostly compile-time sugar

Can be erased or ignored

So:

It’s still fundamentally a tuple

Names are optional metadata

Rust
No named tuples.

You must use:

struct Point { x: i32, y: i32 }
Rust’s stance is effectively:

If you want names, use a struct.

Swift
let p = (x: 3, y: 9)
But:

Type identity is still positional

Names are partially cosmetic

🧠 The deeper philosophical point
The designer is likely asserting:

There are only two real categories:

Tuples → positional, anonymous

Records/structs → named, semantic

And “named tuples” are:

either

tuples with cosmetic labels

or records pretending to be tuples

🧩 Mapping to your earlier language ideas
Your system:

⎛x y⎠
is interesting because:

It is a tuple

But you’re associating names with positions

And allowing destructuring:

a , b ⟵ coord₂
So your design is closer to:

tuples with semantic labels that don’t change positional identity

That’s actually rare.

⚖️ Summary
The claim:

“No programming language has named tuples”

means:

In a strict sense, you must choose:

positional tuples

or named records

but not both simultaneously without changing semantics.

👍 Practical takeaway
Most languages that “have named tuples” are:

either adding labels to tuples (cosmetic)

or providing lightweight structs

Truly combining:

positional semantics

named access

interchangeable usage

is non-trivial and uncommon

🚀 Your design insight (important)
What you’re exploring:

coord₂ ───‣ ⎛x y⎠
plot⎛coord₂ Red 50%⎠
is actually pushing toward:

first-class tuple decomposition + argument splicing + semantic labeling

That goes beyond what most mainstream languages support.

If you want, I can:

classify languages by how “real” their named tuples are

or show how to design a type system that truly supports your model without ambiguity


So this can be added to which languages and how. e.g. Rust via traits, possibly macros; lisp via macros, Forth via words (perhaps), others?  How would you add what is missing to "popular" languages/

Thought for a few seconds
You’re asking how to add the missing pieces:

named tuple bundles

tuple destructuring

argument expansion / splicing

verbs that accept either raw args or bundled args

optionally, arity-signaling names like coord₂, point₄, ÷₂

Most popular languages already have some of this. The hard part is combining all of it cleanly.

Core pattern
Your model can be described as:

coord₂ ───‣ x y
plot   ⟵  x y color transparency ───‣ successˀ

plot(coord₂ Red 50%)
Meaning:

coord₂ expands into two arguments at the call site.
Most languages lack that exact automatic expansion. They usually require either:

plot(coord.x, coord.y, red, alpha)
or:

plot(coord, red, alpha)
or:

1	# AGENTS.md
2	
3	Instructions for AI coding agents (Claude Code, opencode, Cursor, etc.)
4	working in this repository. This project uses **agentrail** to keep
5	multi-session agent work on track. Follow these rules exactly.
6	
7	Rename or copy this file to whatever your agent reads (`AGENTS.md`,
8	`CLAUDE.md`, `.cursorrules`, etc.) -- the content is the same.
9	
10	---
11	
12	## The one-paragraph summary
13	
14	This project uses agentrail to record work as a sequence of **steps** in a
15	**saga**. Each session you run does exactly one step: read the step prompt
16	with `agentrail next`, start it with `agentrail begin`, do the work, commit
17	your changes with git, and close the step with `agentrail complete`. Then
18	stop -- the next step is for the next session. The `.agentrail/` directory
19	is the durable record and must be tracked in git like source code.
20	
21	## The session protocol (follow exactly)
22	
23	### 1. START -- read your instructions
24	
25	```bash
26	agentrail next
27	```
28	
29	This prints the current step's prompt, relevant context files, skill
30	documents, and past successful trajectories for this task type. **Read
31	the entire output carefully.** It is the instruction for this session.
32	
33	If `agentrail next` exits with no current step, the saga is paused or
34	complete -- stop and ask the user what to do. Do not invent work.
35	
36	### 2. BEGIN -- transition the step
37	
38	```bash
39	agentrail begin
40	```
41	
42	This marks the step as `in-progress`. Required before doing work.
43	
44	### 3. WORK -- do exactly what the step prompt says
45	
46	- Do not ask the user "want me to proceed?" or "shall I start?". The
47	  step prompt **is** your instruction. Execute it.
48	- Do not expand scope. If you notice other problems, note them for a
49	  future step -- do not silently fix them in this one.
50	- Stay within the files the step prompt references. If you need to touch
51	  something outside that scope, pause and ask.
52	
53	### 4. COMMIT AND PUSH -- commit your work with git, then push
54	
55	```bash
56	git add <files>
57	git commit -m "<clear message>"
58	git push
59	```
60	
61	**This must happen before `agentrail complete`.** `agentrail complete`
62	captures the current `HEAD` commit hash into the step's `commits` field,
63	which is how future `agentrail audit` runs link the step back to its
64	commit. If you complete before committing, the linkage is wrong.
65	
66	Include `.agentrail/` files you touched in the commit -- they are part of
67	the record.
68	
69	**Every commit must be pushed.** Work that only exists locally is work
70	that can be lost. For every fix or feature commit, run `git push` before
71	moving on. This applies to every repo touched during the step, not just
72	this one -- if you touched `../sw-cor24-pcode` or any sibling, commit
73	and push there too. Don't accumulate a pile of uncommitted or unpushed
74	work and defer it to the end of the session.
75	
76	### 5. COMPLETE -- close the step
77	
78	```bash
79	agentrail complete \
80	  --summary "what you accomplished in one or two sentences" \
81	  --reward 1 \
82	  --actions "tools and approach used"
83	```
84	
85	Flags:
86	- `--reward 1` on success, `--reward -1 --failure-mode "<what-went-wrong>"`
87	  on failure. Reward is used for trajectory recording so future sessions
88	  can learn from what worked.
89	- Add `--done` if this was the last step of the saga.
90	- Use `--next-slug` and `--next-prompt` to define the next step if you
91	  know what it should be; otherwise the human will plan it.
92	
93	### 6. STOP -- do not continue
94	
95	**Do not make any further changes after `agentrail complete`.** Any
96	changes after complete are untracked by the saga and invisible to the
97	next session. If you see more work to do, it belongs in the next step,
98	not this one.
99	
100	---
101	
102	## Rules for `.agentrail/` (CRITICAL -- do not violate)
103	
104	The `.agentrail/` directory is the durable record of saga/step history.
105	Treat it like source code.
106	
107	### Always track it in git
108	
109	- `.agentrail/` **must** be tracked in git. Never add it to `.gitignore`.
110	  If you inherit a repo that has `.agentrail/` ignored, that is a bug --
111	  unignore it and commit the existing contents first.
112	- Commit step artifacts as each step completes, in the same commit as
113	  your code changes.
114	
115	### Never edit or delete files under `.agentrail/` by hand
116	
117	- **Do not** `rm`, `rm -rf`, `mv`, or use `Write`/`Edit` on any file
118	  under `.agentrail/` or `.agentrail-archive/`.
119	- Always go through agentrail subcommands: `init`, `add`, `begin`,
120	  `complete`, `abort`, `archive`, `plan`, `audit`.
121	- Direct deletion of untracked step files is **unrecoverable** -- git
122	  reflog cannot restore blobs that were never staged. This has happened
123	  before and lost saga history.
124	
125	### Commit order matters
126	
127	Work -> `git add` -> `git commit` -> `agentrail complete`. In that order.
128	Completing before committing means `commits` is empty and the audit
129	command can't link step to commit.
130	
131	---
132	
133	## Recovering from gaps
134	
135	If history gets out of sync -- for example, an agent made commits without
136	running `agentrail complete`, or steps were added without matching
137	commits -- use the audit command.
138	
139	```bash
140	agentrail audit                    # human-readable markdown report
141	agentrail audit --emit-commands    # shell script of suggested add lines
142	agentrail audit --since v1.0       # only look at commits after v1.0
143	```
144	
145	The report has four sections:
146	
147	1. **Matched** -- commits that line up with a saga step (by recorded hash
148	   or by timestamp window for legacy steps).
149	2. **Orphan commits** -- commits with no matching step. These are the
150	   gaps.
151	3. **Orphan steps** -- steps whose recorded commit isn't in the current
152	   history (rebased away, squashed, never made).
153	4. **Working tree** -- uncommitted changes. Reported for awareness, not
154	   turned into commands.
155	
156	With `--emit-commands`, the tool prints a shell script with one
157	`agentrail add --commit <hash> --slug ... --prompt ...` line per orphan
158	commit. **Review and edit the slugs and prompts before running** -- the
159	defaults are seeded from commit subjects and need human judgment.
160	
161	## Retroactive history for old projects
162	
163	If the project predates agentrail and you want to add a saga on top of
164	existing history:
165	
166	```bash
167	agentrail audit --emit-commands > rebuild.sh
168	# Edit rebuild.sh: reword slugs and prompts as coherent step descriptions
169	sh rebuild.sh
170	```
171	
172	The script begins with `agentrail init --retroactive --name development`
173	(when no saga exists) and then adds one step per historical commit.
174	Retroactive sagas are marked in `saga.toml` so future audits know those
175	commits are claimed.
176	
177	Going forward from there, run new sagas normally.
178	
179	## Safety net: `agentrail snapshot`
180	
181	If you have files under `.agentrail/` that are not yet committed and
182	you're about to do something risky (a big agent run, a rebase, cleaning
183	up untracked files), run:
184	
185	```bash
186	agentrail snapshot
187	```
188	
189	This creates a git commit under `refs/agentrail/snapshots/<timestamp>`
190	containing a copy of `.agentrail/` and `.agentrail-archive/`. The user's
191	real git index is not touched -- it uses a throwaway temp index under the
192	hood. The snapshot survives `git gc` because a named ref holds it.
193	
194	Restore from a snapshot with a normal git command:
195	
196	```bash
197	git restore --source=refs/agentrail/snapshots/<timestamp> \
198	    -- .agentrail .agentrail-archive
199	```
200	
201	List existing snapshots with `agentrail snapshot --list`.
202	
203	This is a safety net, not a replacement for committing. Commit your
204	work normally -- use snapshot only as belt-and-suspenders insurance.
205	
206	---
207	
208	## Quick reference
209	
210	| Command | When to use |
211	|---|---|
212	| `agentrail next` | Start of every session |
213	| `agentrail begin` | After reading `next`, before working |
214	| `agentrail complete --summary "..." --reward 1` | After committing |
215	| `agentrail status` | Inspect current saga state (read-only) |
216	| `agentrail history` | Show all step summaries (read-only) |
217	| `agentrail plan --update ...` | Revise the saga plan |
218	| `agentrail add --slug ... --prompt ...` | Add a step without completing current one (maintenance mode) |
219	| `agentrail abort --reason "..."` | Mark current step as blocked |
220	| `agentrail archive --reason "..."` | Close out a saga and start fresh |
221	| `agentrail audit` | Diagnose saga-vs-git gaps |
222	| `agentrail snapshot` | Save a safety-net copy of `.agentrail/` into the git object store (opt-in) |
223	| `agentrail snapshot --list` | List existing snapshot refs |
224	
225	## What not to do
226	
227	- Do not run `agentrail complete` before committing.
228	- Do not touch files under `.agentrail/` with anything other than
229	  `agentrail` subcommands.
230	- Do not keep working after `agentrail complete`. Stop and let the next
231	  session pick up.
232	- Do not add `.agentrail/` to `.gitignore`.
233	- Do not skip `agentrail next` "because you remember what the step was"
234	  -- the next output includes trajectories and skill docs that change as
235	  the system learns.
236
